regulationSummary.overview: Iowa has no gold deposits and is not a gold prospecting destination. The state is underlain by Paleozoic limestone and dolomite formations beneath thick glacial drift, with no gold-bearing geology. Iowa's mineral resources consist primarily of limestone, gypsum, coal, and glacial gravel — none of which carry gold. This entry is included for completeness with clear expectations.
keyRules: Iowa does not have a recreational gold prospecting permit system — there are no gold deposits in the state.
Rock and mineral collecting on Iowa DNR public lands may have specific regulations — contact the relevant managing agency.
In-stream work in Iowa waterways requires coordination with the Iowa DNR and Army Corps of Engineers.
regulationSummary.hpaInfo: Not applicable — there are no gold-bearing streams in Iowa.
regulationSummary.suctionDredgeStatus: Not applicable — there are no productive placer gold streams in Iowa.
regulationSummary.handPanning: Gold panning in Iowa streams is not a viable activity. The state has no gold-bearing geology. Iowa is a good destination for fossil collecting (particularly crinoids and brachiopods in Devonian limestone outcrops) but not gold prospecting.
regulationSummary.disclaimer: Regulations change. Verify current rules at the official sources listed below before going out.

officialResources.title: Iowa Geological Survey
officialResources.description: State geological survey — Iowa has no gold-bearing geology. The survey covers Iowa's actual mineral resources (limestone, gypsum, coal).

officialResources.title: Iowa DNR — Lands and Waters
officialResources.description: Iowa Department of Natural Resources — manages state public lands and waterways.
